explicit_text: I was born in Umpiem Refugee Camp in Thailand in 2000. My family had fled there from Burma in 1999 because the Democratic Karen Buddhist Army burned down our village. Life in Umpiem was difficult, and I only reached first grade before my mom decided we should move to the United States. The camp was a place of refuge but not much opportunity for education or growth.

explicit_text: Thailand was where I was born and where my family sought refuge after escaping the war in Burma. We lived in Umpiem Refugee Camp there, and I experienced my early childhood in Thailand. In 2008, we traveled through Thailand, stopping in Mae Sot and Bangkok before flying to the United States. Thailand was both a place of safety and transition for us.

explicit_text: Burma is where my parents, sister, and grandparents were born and where our family lived before the war forced us to flee. In 1999, the Democratic Karen Buddhist Army burned down our village, which made us leave Burma and move to Thailand. Although I was born after we left, Burma is the place my family calls home and the reason for our difficult journey.

explicit_text: The Democratic Karen Buddhist Army played a tragic role in my family's history. In 1999, they burned down our village in Burma, which forced my parents and grandparents to flee to Thailand. Their actions directly caused us to become refugees and shaped the path of my early life. This group was a reason for the hardship and displacement my family endured.

explicit_text: Mae Sot was one of the first stops on our journey from Thailand to the United States in 2008. We waited there for about five hours, and I remember seeing people selling food on the street, which smelled so good that my mom bought meatballs for me and my sister. It was my first time riding in a taxi and traveling so far, and Mae Sot was a brief but memorable part of that trip.

explicit_text: Bangkok was another important stop during our journey in 2008 before we flew to the United States. We waited there for about an hour, and I saw many other refugee families waiting for taxis. The hotel staff kindly gave us rice soup, which tasted delicious to me. Bangkok was a place of rest and kindness amidst our long travel.

explicit_text: Our first arrival in the United States was through New York City in 2008. After flying from Bangkok, we landed there and then took another plane to Georgia. I was very tired on the plane and tried the food, though I didn’t like it. New York was the gateway to our new life in America.
implicit_text: Our first arrival in the United States was through a major city on the East Coast in 2008. After flying from Bangkok, we landed there and then took another plane to Georgia. I was very tired on the plane and tried the food, though I didn’t like it. That city was the gateway to our new life in America.

explicit_text: My family first settled in Georgia when we arrived in the United States in 2008. We lived there for three years in a big brick apartment, which was very different from the bamboo and leaves of the refugee camp. I was happy there, especially because the apartment had three big rooms and food like fried rice and chicken wings in the fridge. However, we eventually moved to Minnesota because Georgia didn’t have good medical care for us.
implicit_text: My family first settled in a southeastern U.S. state when we arrived in the United States in 2008. explicit_text: When we arrived in Georgia, we landed at Hartsfield–Jackson Atlanta International Airport. I remember getting off the plane and seeing our caseworker who took us to our new apartment. It was a big moment for us because it marked the beginning of our new life in America. The airport was the first place where I felt we had truly arrived.

It was a big moment for us because it marked the beginning of our new life in America. The airport was the first place where I felt we had truly arrived.

explicit_text: After living in Georgia for three years, my family moved to Minnesota because we needed better medical care. Minnesota has given me a good opportunity to get an education and improve my life. It feels like a place where I can grow and have a better future compared to where we started. Moving here was an important step for our family’s well-being.

explicit_text: The United States became my family's new home starting in 2008 when we resettled here after fleeing Burma and living in refugee camps in Thailand. We first lived in Georgia, then moved to Minnesota for better medical care and education. The US has given me opportunities I never had before, like going to school and living in a strong brick apartment. It represents hope and a fresh start for me and my family.
